headline: Geopolitical Shockwave Rattles Monday Session as Chip Stocks Bear the Brunt
body_html:
Monday's session closed on a sour note as renewed tensions in the Middle East drove broad risk aversion across Wall Street. President Trump's announcement reinstating an Iran blockade — and a proposed 20% levy on cargo transiting the Strait of Hormuz — rattled sentiment from the opening bell, sending decliners outnumbering advancers 223 to 169 across our 396-name universe. The threat to a critical global shipping artery proved the session's defining catalyst.

The damage was concentrated and severe in semiconductors, which shed 5.16% as a group — easily the worst-performing sector of the day. Memory, connectivity, and power-chip names led losses, with several names shedding between roughly 8% and 13%. Homebuilders and materials also retreated, while energy surged 1.41% as oil-supply concerns pushed refining names sharply higher. Software added 0.66%, demonstrating modest defensive appeal as money rotated away from high-beta technology.

The standout individual mover was Gartner, which climbed more than 6% to top the day's leaderboard — a notable divergence from the broader selloff. Valero Energy's 5.38% advance underscored how geopolitical risk, which punished most of the tape, simultaneously rewarded corners of the energy complex. Until clarity emerges on the Hormuz situation, expect volatility to remain an unwelcome fixture.

briefs.headline: Trump Reinstates Iran Blockade, Proposes Hormuz Toll
briefs.body: President Trump confirmed the United States will begin enforcing a maritime blockade on Iran starting Tuesday and floated a 20% charge on cargo passing through the Strait of Hormuz. The announcements blindsided markets, immediately pressuring risk assets while lifting energy-related names on supply-disruption concerns.

briefs.headline: Semiconductor Sector Suffers Steepest Decline of the Session
briefs.body: Chipmakers were Monday's most punished cohort, dropping 5.16% as a group. Sandisk fell 12.63%, Nextracker shed 10.61% in the adjacent solar-equipment space, and names across memory, connectivity, and power segments all posted significant single-session losses, reflecting investor anxiety over supply-chain exposure to the region.

briefs.headline: Energy and Refining Names Rally on Supply-Risk Premium
briefs.body: While most sectors retreated, energy gained 1.41% as the prospect of Hormuz disruptions lifted crude-sensitive names. Valero Energy rose 5.38%, ranking among the session's top performers and illustrating how the same geopolitical headlines that pressured equities broadly created distinct pockets of outperformance in refining.

briefs.headline: Gartner Surges More Than 6%, Leading All Gainers
briefs.body: Research and advisory firm Gartner posted the day's largest advance at 6.06%, standing out sharply against a broadly negative tape. The move highlighted investor appetite for subscription-based information businesses perceived as insulated from geopolitical and industrial-cycle pressures that weighed on hardware and commodity-linked names.

briefs.headline: UN Maritime Agency Pushes Back on Strait Transit Fees
briefs.body: The United Nations' shipping authority voiced opposition to any fees imposed on vessels transiting international straits following the Trump administration's Hormuz toll proposal. The stance underscores the diplomatic friction the plan has generated and raises questions about enforcement mechanics and multilateral compliance.

briefs.headline: Chipotle Charts International Expansion With Mexico Entry
briefs.body: Chipotle Mexican Grill announced plans to open its first location in Mexico, marking a significant symbolic and strategic milestone for the fast-casual chain. The move extends the brand's footprint into the very culinary tradition that inspired its menu, testing whether its model can resonate in the market that defined its cuisine.
